bio.extra_names: Wilfred or William Langdon Kihn; signed paintings 'W. Langdon Kihn'; Blackfeet name 'Zoi-och-ka-tsai-ya' (Chase Enemy in Water).
bio.photo_notes: Photo provided by Sam Hooker (project compiler).

burial.plot_notes: Cove Cemetery, Hadlyme, New London County CT. FindAGrave Memorial #135411069.

notable.notable_blurb: Native American portrait painter, National Geographic illustrator

education.notes: Recognized for artistic talent at Boys' High School in Brooklyn.

education.school_name: Art Students League of New York
education.notes: Pupil of Homer Boss and Winold Reiss. Reiss's influence on Indian portraiture decisively shaped Kihn's career direction.

career.role: Portrait painter (Native American subjects) and illustrator
career.notes: From 1935 onward, occupied with a pictorial survey in oils of the Indian tribes commissioned by the National Geographic Society — a project that took him from the Atlantic to the Pacific and from Mexico to the Arctic. Also illustrated books including Marius Barbeau's 'Indian Days in the Canadian Rockies' (1923), Frances Carpenter's 'Pocahontas and Her World' (1961), and the children's story 'Flat Tail' by Alice Gall and Fleming Crew (1935). Painted Nathan Hale murals at Hale-Ray High School in Moodus (East Haddam).

narrative_blocks.header: Brooklyn boy who painted the disappearing tribes
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Brooklyn September 5, 1898, son of Alfred Charles Kihn and Carrie Lowe Peck. He attended Boys' High School in Brooklyn where his artistic talent was recognized, and studied 1916–17 at the Art Students League of New York under Homer Boss and Winold Reiss. Reiss's interest in Native American portraiture decisively shaped his career; from 1920 onward Kihn lived intermittently in the Western United States and Canada, documenting tribal cultures he believed were vanishing.

narrative_blocks.header: Admitted to the Blackfeet — Chase Enemy in Water
narrative_blocks.body: In 1920 the Blackfeet tribe in Montana adopted him as a member, giving him the name 'Zoi-och-ka-tsai-ya' — Chase Enemy in Water. Many of his portraits afterward bore a pictogram of that name alongside his signature. The New York Times in 1922 described his portraits as marvels of incisive characterization, recording the subtle differences of aspect difficult enough to discern in races other than our own.

narrative_blocks.header: National Geographic's tribal survey, Atlantic to Arctic
narrative_blocks.body: From 1935 on, his principal work was a pictorial survey in oils of the Indian tribes commissioned by the National Geographic Society. The project took him from the Atlantic to the Pacific and from Mexico to the Arctic, and produced many of the colored portraits that ran in the magazine through the late 1930s and 1940s. He also illustrated books for Marius Barbeau and Frances Carpenter.

narrative_blocks.header: Tenniel-style Alice murals for his daughter, 1934
narrative_blocks.body: He married Helen Van Tine Butler of Meriden in 1920 and settled in Hadlyme, Connecticut. In 1934 he painted Alice in Wonderland murals after the Tenniel style on the walls of his daughter Phyllis's dormered bedroom; the inscription above the bed reads 'A ma chere Phyllis, with fond affection these walls are respectfully inscribed. W.

Along with writer Donald Barr Chidsey, he stood as a Democratic candidate for the Connecticut House of Representatives from the town of Lyme in the election of November 2, 1948. He continued painting and illustrating until his final illness; he died at Lawrence Memorial Hospital in New London on December 12, 1957, aged fifty-nine.

sources.label: New York Times — 'Portraits of Native Americans' Wesleyan University Davison Art Center exhibition

burial.plot_notes: Cove Cemetery, Hadlyme CT. FindAGrave Memorial #135411204.

narrative_blocks.header: Slept under her father's Tenniel-style Alice murals
narrative_blocks.body: Born February 3, 1924 in New York, only child of W. Langdon Kihn and Helen Van Tine Butler. The dormered bedroom of her Hadlyme childhood home was painted with Alice in Wonderland murals her father gave her in 1934, signed 'A ma chere Phyllis, with fond affection.' She lived at East Haddam through her father's career and devoted herself after his death to keeping his legacy alive — quoted at length in Wesleyan University's 1987 Davison Art Center exhibition of his Native American portraits.

burial.plot_notes: First Church Cemetery, East Haddam CT. FindAGrave Memorial #109788831.
First Church Cemetery, East Haddam CT. FindAGrave Memorial #109788845.
Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #12917848.

career.notes: President of Home National Bank of Meriden — a substantial Meriden banking institution.

military_service.notes: Served in Company C, Seventh Regiment Connecticut Volunteers. The 7th CT fought in the Department of the South — Hilton Head, Fort Wagner, Olustee, Bermuda Hundred, Cold Harbor — and the regiment served alongside the 54th Massachusetts at Fort Wagner. Specific dates and battles for Butler personally not yet researched.

cross_connections.display_label: 16th Connecticut Volunteers — Antietam, left Yale to enlist
cross_connections.notes: Two Connecticut Volunteers in the same war. Edwin Howell Butler served in Company C, Seventh Connecticut Volunteers in the Department of the South — Hilton Head, Fort Wagner, Olustee, Bermuda Hundred. Arthur De Neufville Talcott (Hooker descendant on the Talcott side) mustered into Company A, Sixteenth Connecticut Volunteers in July 1862 — leaving Yale College in his freshman year — and was wounded at the Battle of Antietam two months later, fighting in the same 40-acre cornfield mentioned in the Bauer letters that document Nathaniel Lyman Bradley's domestic life.

narrative_blocks.body: He served in Company C, Seventh Regiment Connecticut Volunteers during the Civil War. The Seventh fought in the Department of the South — Fort Wagner, Olustee, Bermuda Hundred — and served alongside the 54th Massachusetts at the assault on Fort Wagner. Specific battle service for Butler not yet researched.

narrative_blocks.header: President of Home National Bank of Meriden
narrative_blocks.body: After the war he returned to Meriden and entered banking, rising to the presidency of Home National Bank. He died at New Haven on December 21, 1894, aged fifty, and was buried at Walnut Grove Cemetery in Meriden.

bio.extra_names: EH #2832 records as 'Harriet Isabel'; FindAGrave and her own gravestone use 'Harriet Isabella'.
bio.photo_notes: Photo provided by Sam Hooker (project compiler).

burial.plot_notes: Walnut Grove Cemetery, Meriden CT. FindAGrave Memorial #12917851.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name honors her older sister Isabella Norton, who died at age nine three years before her birth.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for an older sister dead at nine
narrative_blocks.body: Born July 5, 1843 at Kensington, second daughter of Capt. Albert Norton and Ruth Hart. Her sister Isabella had died at Kensington on December 15, 1840 aged nine, and her parents gave her the middle name Isabella to carry it forward. Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy abbreviated it to 'Isabel' in EH 2832, but her gravestone and surviving records preserve the full form.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Civil War veteran and Meriden banker
narrative_blocks.body: She became the second wife of Edwin Howell Butler of Meriden, Civil War veteran of Company C of the Seventh Connecticut Volunteers and later president of Home National Bank. He had been previously married to Marie L. Brown, who died in 1874.
